name: MXFP8 Quantization Optimization
trigger: MXFP8, FP8, quantize, dequantize, block_scale, microscaling, E4M3, E8M0, mixed precision, int8, fp4
approach: MXFP8 uses FP8E4M3 elements with FP8E8M0 scale factors applied per 32-element block. Key optimizations: (1) Fuse quantization into kernel prologues/epilogues to avoid separate quant pass — quantization overhead can be 76% of matmul time. (2) Produce scale factors in hardware-compatible layout (tcgen05.mma expects specific TMEM layout). (3) Use hardware block-scaled MMA on Blackwell (tcgen05.mma...block_scale) which handles dequantization in tensor cores. (4) On Hopper, pipeline dequantization with CUDA cores while tensor cores run next MMA. (5) For quantization kernels, target 6+ TB/s bandwidth on B200 using cp.async.bulk (intra-SM instructions), avoid TMA swizzling overhead. (6) Fuse non-transposed + transposed output for backward pass in single quantization kernel.

evidence.strategy: utilize hardware-native instructions and specialized tensor cores to accelerate kernel execution.

pitfalls: Quantization overhead can be 76% of matmul time on Blackwell if not fused — always fuse quant into kernel prologue/epilogue
TransformerEngine/TorchAO quantization kernels only achieve 4.5 TB/s — custom kernels can reach 6.2+ TB/s using cp.async.bulk
MXFP8 scale factor layout for tcgen05.mma is unintuitive (not simple M x N/32) — producing wrong layout requires reshaping which kills performance
On Blackwell, CUDA core FP32 throughput (80 TFLOPS) is 1/56th of FP8 tensor core throughput (4500 TFLOPS) — dequantization in CUDA cores takes 1.76x the matmul time
32-block scaling means absolute_max is computed per 32 elements, cast_to_fp8e8m0 rounds UP to nearest power of 2 and min-clamps to 2^-127
Fused quantization kernel should output both non-transposed and transposed results for forward+backward pass efficiency
MXFP8 training loss matches BF16 within 0.04% when using E4M3 elements + E8M0 scales + round-to-nearest-even
